%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.7-2 Time-Invariant and Time-Varying Systems

> **Section Objective:** Decide whether a system behaves the same after a time shift, or whether its rule changes with time.

---

### CONCEPTS IN THIS SECTION

- Time-invariant system
- Time-varying system
- Delay-commutation test
- Constant-coefficient LTI system
- Representative time-invariance tests

## 1. Time Invariance Means Delay Does Not Change the Rule

A system is **time-invariant** when the same input shape, delayed by \(T\) seconds, produces the same output shape delayed by exactly \(T\) seconds. The system's parameters do not change with time — the system does not care where the signal is placed on the time axis.

**Representative example:** If an audio processing system is time-invariant, delaying the audio input by 2 seconds delays the processed audio by 2 seconds, without changing the processing itself. The filter, gain, or effect applied is identical regardless of when the audio arrives.

> **Exam Note:** On exams, test whether delay and the system can be swapped without changing the final signal. If 'delay then system' equals 'system then delay,' the system is time-invariant.

## 2. Time-Varying Systems Depend on the Clock

This is the core time-invariance formula. Here \(S\) is the system, \(x(t)\) is the original input, \(y(t)\) is the original output, and \(T\) is the delay in seconds.

**When to use it:** Whenever a problem asks whether a system is time-invariant, apply this test.

**Exam trigger:** Compare 'delay input then apply \(S\)' with 'apply \(S\) then delay output.' Both paths must produce the same final signal.

**Common misuse:** Students often delay only \(x(t)\) but forget that every occurrence of \(t\) in the output expression must shift consistently. When checking \(y(t-T)\), replace \(t\) with \(t-T\) everywhere in the output formula.

$$x(t) \xrightarrow{\,S\,} y(t) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x(t-T) \xrightarrow{\,S\,} y(t-T)$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. 1.28](/figures/page-102-fig__1_28-1.png)
*Fig. 1.28 shows the time-invariance test: delaying the input before the system must match delaying the output after the system.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure illustrates two signal paths side by side. In the first path, the original input \(x(t)\) passes through system \(S\) to produce \(y(t)\), and then a delay block shifts it to \(y(t-T)\). In the second path, \(x(t)\) is first delayed to \(x(t-T)\) and then passed through system \(S\), also producing \(y(t-T)\). Signal plots show that the delayed waveforms keep the same shape but are shifted right by \(T\) on the time axis. Students should notice that for a time-invariant system, both paths end at the identical waveform \(y(t-T)\) — the shape is preserved and only its time location shifts.</div>

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 3. Representative Test: Two Systems

A time-varying system has a rule or parameter that **changes with \(t\)**. Consider the textbook example \(S[x](t) = e^{-t}x(t)\). Even if the input shape is merely delayed, the multiplier \(e^{-t}\) is evaluated at the **current clock time**, not at the shifted time. So the output is not just a delayed copy of the old output — the amplitude envelope changes depending on when the signal actually arrives.

> **Exam Warning:** Seeing \(x(t-T)\) in both paths is not enough. You must compare the **entire output expression**, including any time-dependent coefficients. A coefficient like \(e^{-t}\), \(t\), \(\cos(t)\), or \(u(t)\) multiplying the input is a strong signal of time variation.

$$e^{-(t-T)}x(t-T) \ne e^{-t}x(t-T)$$
*For the system \(y(t) = e^{-t}x(t)\), the two time-invariance test paths give different results:

- **Left side** \(e^{-(t-T)}x(t-T)\): the delayed version of the original output — replace \(t\) with \(t-T\) everywhere in \(y(t)\).
- **Right side** \(e^{-t}x(t-T)\): the output after delaying the input first and then applying the system — the multiplier \(e^{-t}\) still uses the current clock time \(t\).

Because \(e^{-(t-T)} = e^{-t}e^{T} \ne e^{-t}\) (unless \(T=0\)), the two sides differ. The system is **time-varying**.

**Exam trigger:** A coefficient like \(e^{-t}\), \(t\), \(\cos(t)\), or \(u(t)\) multiplying the input almost always signals time variation.

**Common misuse:** Stopping after confirming both sides contain \(x(t-T)\) and ignoring the extra coefficient difference.*

## 3. Representative Test: Two Systems

Based on Example 1.11, here are two mini-cases that illustrate the test.

---

### CASE A — Time-Varying: \(y(t) = x(t)u(t)\)

Choose \(x(t) = 1\) and delay \(T = 1\).

- **Path 1 (system then delay):** Apply the system to get \(y(t) = u(t)\), then delay: \(y(t-1) = u(t-1)\). The step turns on at \(t = 1\).
- **Path 2 (delay then system):** Delay the input to get \(x(t-1) = 1\), then apply the system: \(y = 1 \cdot u(t) = u(t)\). The step still turns on at \(t = 0\).

The two paths give \(u(t-1) \ne u(t)\). The switching point does **not** move with the delayed input. The system is **time-varying**.

---

### CASE B — Time-Invariant: \(y(t) = \frac{d}{dt}x(t)\)

- **Path 1 (system then delay):** Differentiate \(x(t)\) to get \(x'(t)\), then delay: \(x'(t-T)\).
- **Path 2 (delay then system):** Delay the input to get \(x(t-T)\), then differentiate: \(\frac{d}{dt}x(t-T) = x'(t-T)\).

Both paths give \(x'(t-T)\). The derivative operation **commutes** with a time delay. The system is **time-invariant**.

---

> **Quick Check:** If you can find even one value of \(T\) or one input \(x(t)\) for which the two paths differ, the system is **not** time-invariant.

### Common Trap: time scaling is usually not time-invariant

A system can fail time invariance even if it has no explicit \(t\). Example:

$$S[x](t)=x(2t)$$

Path 1, system then delay:
$$y(t-T)=x(2(t-T))=x(2t-2T)$$

Path 2, delay input then system:
$$S[x(t-T)](t)=x(2t-T)$$

These are not the same for \(T\ne0\), so \(x(2t)\) is time-varying by the delay test.

Another common trap is fixed-limit integration:

$$y(t)=\int_0^t x(\tau)d\tau$$

The lower limit is tied to the fixed origin \(0\), so delaying the input does not simply delay the output.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- **Time-invariance test:** \(x(t) \xrightarrow{S} y(t)\) implies \(x(t-T) \xrightarrow{S} y(t-T)\) — delay and system must commute.
- **Time-varying failure:** For \(y(t)=e^{-t}x(t)\), the two paths give \(e^{-(t-T)}x(t-T) \ne e^{-t}x(t-T)\) — the time-dependent multiplier breaks commutativity.
- **LTI indicator:** A linear differential-equation system with **constant** coefficients \(a_i\) and \(b_i\) is LTI; time-dependent coefficients make it time-varying.
- **Counterexample rule:** One input or one delay \(T\) for which the two paths differ is sufficient to prove a system is time-varying.

*Next, use these classification tests together to recognize broader system types quickly.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
